answers.intake_q1: Can you describe the chest pain? For example, is it sharp, dull, pressure-like, or burning?
answers.intake_q2: When did the chest pain start?
answers.intake_q3: Where exactly is the chest pain located?
answers.intake_q4: Any shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or pain radiating to your arm or jaw?
answers.intake_q5: Have you noticed any swelling, redness, or tenderness in your chest or nearby areas?
answers.intake_a5: tenderness when I press on it
answers.intake_q6: Have you had any recent trauma or injury to your chest area?
answers.intake_a6: not that I'm aware of but right before this began I was helping a friend move and was carrying a lot  of heavy boxes
answers.intake_q7: Any history of heart disease, lung problems, or blood clots?
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or recent respiratory infections?
answers.intake_summary: A patient presents with one week of sharp, localized chest pain next to the sternum, tender to palpation, without systemic symptoms or cardiac risk factors. The pain started after heavy lifting. This is consistent with musculoskeletal chest pain.

gating_notes: liver disease status is yes/unknown (avoid hepatotoxic meds / adjust dosing)

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with one week of sharp chest pain localized next to the sternum. The pain is tender to palpation and began after heavy lifting while helping a friend move. She denies shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, pain radiating to the arm or jaw, fever, chills, or recent respiratory infections. There is no history of heart disease, lung problems, or blood clots. The clinical picture is consistent with musculoskeletal chest pain, likely costochondritis. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
Working diagnosis: Costochondritis, characterized by localized sharp chest pain tender to palpation, with onset after physical strain. 
Differential: 
1. Costochondritis 
2. Muscular strain or chest wall injury 
3. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) 
4. Cardiac ischemia (less likely given absence of risk factors and symptoms)

P: 
- - Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain, for 7-10 days; avoid if liver disease worsens.
- Alternatives: 
 - Ibuprofen 400-600 mg orally every 6-8 hours as needed for pain and inflammation, for 7-10 days. Avoid if history of NSAID allergy or active gastrointestinal disease. Dosing details were not found in retrieved evidence; prescriber should verify. (avoid in renal disease; especially severe CKD/dialysis)
 - Topical NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac gel) applied to the affected area 3-4 times daily for 7-10 days; avoid if skin irritation occurs. 
- Medication safety considerations: The patient has liver disease; avoid or use caution with acetaminophen and NSAIDs, monitoring for hepatotoxicity. No pregnancy or renal disease concerns. No known medication allergies. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek urgent care if chest pain worsens, becomes constant, is associated with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, or radiation to the arm or jaw. 
- Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing/workup as indicated.

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Am Fam Physician. 2021;104(1):73-78 Patient information: See related handout on costochondritis. Author disclosure: No relevant financial affiliations. Costochondritis is a common cause of chest pain. It most commonly occurs in adults between 40 and 50 years of age, with a slight predominance in women. Although musculoskeletal and other chest wall conditions…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Costochondritis is inflammation where your ribs join the bone in the middle of your chest (breastbone). It can cause sharp chest pain, especially when moving or breathing. It usually gets better on its own over time. Symptoms of costochondritis Costochondritis may cause sharp pain in the front or side of your chest. The pain may be worse when: - moving your…
